input_candidates.truth_value: The review emphasizes that Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel can achieve tensile strength up to 1.1 GPa with ductility ≥40%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: General density range of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel: 6.2-7.0 g cm^-3 with Al addition from 2 to 12 wt%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Alloying design: C addition reduces density most strongly (4 times that of Al), but solid solubility limit of C in austenite is below 1.2 wt% to avoid crack formation; Al is considered the most suitable alloying element for both density reduction and yield strength improvement.
input_candidates.prediction_value: General mechanical property ranges reported: yield strength up to 950 MPa, tensile strength up to 1.1 GPa, plastic elongation not less than 40%.

input_candidates.truth_value: For Fe-17Mn-0.45C-2Al, Fe-19Mn-0.45C-2Al, and Fe-22Mn-0.45C-2Al steels at cryogenic temperature, ductility decreased to 75.7%, 53.4% and 9.7% of room-temperature values, and Charpy impact energy decreased to 80.5%, 49.8% and 41.1%, respectively. Martensite formed in the 17Mn steel.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Phase decomposition sequence in Fe-Mn-C-Al: γ → γ′ + γ″ → γ′ + L1₂(SRO) → γ + κ, occurring at 400-650 °C for 0.5-100 h within Mn: 12-30 wt%, Al: up to 12 wt%, C: 0.6-2.0 wt% range.

input_candidates.truth_value: For Fe-18Mn-0.6C-xAl steels at cryogenic temperature: impact energy was 122 J for 3Al, 138 J for 5Al, but only 18 J for 8Al due to δ-ferrite formation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: κ precipitation occurs in high Al-containing steel (≥7 wt%), while Fe(Mn)₃C carbide is commonly observed in low Al-containing steel (<7 wt%).

input_candidates.truth_value: Room-temperature Charpy impact energy of 22Mn steel was 149 J vs 44 J for 18Mn steel; Mn stabilizes austenite and improves low-temperature toughness.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Challenges for industrial application: low heat dissipation rate of high-Mn ingots and Al-oxide pollution during casting and rolling.

input_candidates.truth_value: Wear behavior: For Fe-22.33Mn-1.35C-9.87Al, aging at 600°C for 3 h produced good wear resistance with incoherent κ inhibiting crack nucleation. For Fe-28Mn-1C-8.5Al, overaging at 550°C for >2 h caused coarse κ along grain boundaries, leading to microcracking and delamination.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Empirical density formula: ρ = 8.15 - 0.101(wt%Al) - 0.41(wt%C) - 0.0085(wt%Mn).

input_candidates.truth_value: MIM-processed Fe-22Mn-0.4C-1.5Al-1.5Si steel sintered at 1360°C produced fully austenitic matrix with uniformly distributed manganese oxide particles (Figure 9).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Empirical lattice constant formula: α = 0.35721 + 0.0002771(wt%Al) + 0.0044089(wt%C) + 0.0001166(wt%Mn).

input_candidates.truth_value: Hydrogen embrittlement: Al addition mitigates HE by increasing hydrogen solubility and reducing diffusivity.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Yield strength empirical relations: σγ = 10.5 Al_wt% + 351.5; σγ = 0.58 Mn_wt% + 328.0; σγ = 180.7 C_wt% + 250.7.

input_candidates.truth_value: Production challenges include low heat dissipation of high-Mn ingots, Al-oxide pollution during casting and rolling, and chemical inhomogeneity; solutions such as CaO-SiO₂, CaO-Al₂O₃ mold fluxes and B₂O₃-Li₂O-BaO fluxing agents are being explored.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Dislocation slip mode parameter: W_R = γ/μ - 2(1+ν)b_eΩc/π(1-ν), indicating stronger planar slipping with increasing Al content.

input_candidates.truth_value: Future directions: optimize aging to balance strength/ductility with κ precipitation; reduce Al and Mn usage to lower cost; develop hot stamping technology for forming accuracy.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table 1 summary: Al reduces density but weakens TWIP; Nb improves yield stress but weakens TWIP; C strengthens twinning but causes unpredictable shear fracture; Mn improves cryogenic properties but offers little room-temperature benefit.

input_candidates.truth_value: Phase composition: fully austenitic steels require Mn 12-30 wt%, Al 5-12 wt%, C 0.6-1.2 wt%; austenite-based duplex steels have Mn 5-30 wt%, Al 5-10 wt%, C 0.4-0.7 wt%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: For Fe-xMn-0.45C-2Al steel, Charpy impact energy at room temperature: 149 J for 22 wt% Mn steel and 44 J for 18 wt% Mn steel.

input_candidates.truth_value: Isothermal phase sections at 900°C (Figure 2) show that with increasing Mn, γ region expands in high Al area (>8 wt%), κ phase region expands at high C (>3 wt%) and high Al (>10 wt%); M3C and κ phases appear above 1.2 wt% C; α phase region expands with high Al.
input_candidates.prediction_value: For Fe-xMn-0.45C-2Al steel at cryogenic temperature, ductility retained 75.7%, 53.4%, and 9.7% of room temperature values for 17, 19, and 22 wt% Mn steels respectively; impact energy retained 80.5%, 49.8%, and 41.1%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Aging sequence for γ decomposition (valid for Mn 12-30%, Al ≤12%, C 0.6-2.0 wt%): γ → γ' + γ'' → γ' + L1₂ (SRO) → γ + κ. Slow cooling (<4.4°C/h) causes κ precipitation in Fe-28Mn-8.5Al-1C-1.2Si steel.
input_candidates.prediction_value: For Fe-18Mn-0.6C-xAl steel at cryogenic temperature, impact energy increased from 122 J (3 wt% Al) to 138 J (5 wt% Al), but dropped to 18 J at 8 wt% Al due to δ-ferrite formation.

input_candidates.truth_value: κ-carbide precipitates in high Al steels (≥7 wt%); Fe(Mn)₃C dominates in low Al steels (<7 wt%). Overaging coarsens κ particles and reduces work hardening capacity.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Wear rate data for Fe-22.33Mn-1.35C-9.87Al: as-homogenized 0.35 mm^3/Nm; after aging at 500 °C for 1-3 h: 0.29, 0.24, 0.21 mm^3/Nm; at 550 °C: 0.24, 0.19, 0.17 mm^3/Nm; at 600 °C: 0.18, 0.14, 0.11 mm^3/Nm.

input_candidates.truth_value: TWIP effect is suppressed by Al addition (6 wt% Al eliminates TWIP in Fe-22Mn-0.6C-6Al steel) due to increased SFE; TWIP is mainly active in low Al steels.
input_candidates.prediction_value: TRIP-effect low-density steel can reach 950 MPa tensile strength with optimized cold rolling of 45% reduction.

input_candidates.truth_value: TRIP effect operates in austenite-based duplex steels with C < 0.5 wt% and Al 3-6 wt%, where retained austenite transforms to martensite under strain, delaying necking.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Necking strains by phase: martensite 0.5, bainite 0.127, ferrite 0.163, retained austenite 0.348 (from ABAQUS/UMAT simulation).

input_candidates.truth_value: SRO effect promotes planar dislocation slip in high Al (≥6 wt%) steels; dislocation substructures (Taylor lattices, cells) contribute to work hardening.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fe-22Mn-0.6C-6Al steel shows approximately 9% density reduction but TWIP effect is eliminated.

input_candidates.truth_value: Alloying effects summary (Table 1): Al reduces density but weakens TWIP; Nb improves yield stress but weakens TWIP; C enhances twinning but may cause shear fracture via DSA; Mn improves cryogenic toughness but has little effect at room temperature.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Hydrogen embrittlement sensitivity in Fe-Mn-C-Al steel is mitigated by Al addition through enhanced hydrogen solubility and reduced hydrogen diffusivity.

candidates.truth_value: κ-carbide precipitates in high Al steels (≥7 wt%); Fe(Mn)₃C dominates in low Al steels (<7 wt%). Overaging coarsens κ particles and reduces work hardening capacity.
